name: Animo
classification_notes: Animo is a display typeface derived from the distorted letterforms produced by repeated photographic enlargement of metal type, giving it a rough, analogue character rooted in mid-20th century Spanish sign-making tradition — placing it firmly in the vernacular display category.
summary: A display typeface drawn from the distorted letterforms of mid-20th century Spanish sign-making, where repeated photographic enlargement of metal type produced unintended but distinctive aberrations.
description: Designed by Jan Horčík and Arnaud Chemin, Animo traces its origins to Spanish business signage produced in the latter half of the 20th century, where sign-makers used iterative photographic enlargement of small metal type, each pass accumulating optical distortions. The result is a family of 11 styles across 356 languages and 1,396 glyphs that preserves the charm of that analogue process without mimicking it literally. It sits naturally in editorial, branding and display contexts that call for cultural texture and historical grounding.
foundry_blurb: Heavyweight describe Animo as a typeface inspired by old Spanish business signs still visible across cities and towns, created by sign-making companies in the second half of the 20th century using a process of repeatedly photographing and enlarging small metal type. Each iteration introduced new distortions and aberrations which, though purely practical in intent, produced letterforms of unexpected beauty. Animo draws from these imperfections, blending cultural history with the charm of an imperfect, analogue process.

name: Clin
classification_notes: Clin is grounded in the American grotesque tradition, drawing from Franklin Gothic's skeleton while reducing shading and contrast. It incorporates neo-grotesque discipline — balanced proportions and restrained details — placing it firmly in the grotesque sans-serif subcategory.
summary: A contemporary grotesque drawing on the Franklin Gothic skeleton, reduced in shading and softened in contrast to perform evenly across body text and large-scale display.
description: Designed by Filip Matejíček and Jan Charvát at Heavyweight, Clin strips back the historical shading of American grotesque to produce straighter strokes and more moderate contrast. The result holds up at small sizes and reads cleanly in heavy black headlines without tipping into either direction. Neo-grotesque principles of balanced proportions and disciplined metrics are folded in, keeping mannerism out of the equation across its 10 styles and 35 language coverage.
foundry_blurb: Heavyweight describe Clin as a contemporary interpretation of the Franklin Gothic tradition, built on the skeleton of historical American grotesque but deliberately less shaded. Straighter strokes and moderate contrast keep it stable at small sizes and in large black headlines alike, while borrowed neo-grotesque principles — balanced proportions, disciplined metrics and restrained details — ensure legibility without mannerism.

name: Beaujon
designer: Connor Davenport, Luke Charsley, Léa Bruneau
classification_notes: Beaujon is an old-style roman referencing French Renaissance printing types, specifically Parmentier's Garamond and Vendome by Ganeau, placing it squarely in the Garalde/humanist old-style tradition.
summary: A French Renaissance serif drawing on Beatrice Warde's attribution research into the Garamond types, referencing Parmentier's Garamond and Vendome across Display and Text optical sizes.
description: Designed by Connor Davenport, Luke Charsley and Léa Bruneau, Beaujon takes its name from the pseudonym under which Beatrice Warde published her 1926 essay challenging the attribution of the so-called Garamond types to Claude Garamont. The family spans 22 styles across multiple weights with Display and Text optical sizes, built with variable fonts in mind, covering 282 languages and 956 glyphs. Its formal references run from Parmentier's Garamond revival to Ganeau's mid-century Vendome, placing it within a century-long tradition of French Renaissance reinterpretation rather than any single historical source.
foundry_blurb: Heavyweight describe Beaujon as an homage to French Renaissance printing types and the research of Beatrice Warde, referencing Parmentier's Garamond and Vendome by Ganeau. The family continues the legacy of its predecessors across a comprehensive range of weights and optical sizes, Display and Text, and was designed with variable fonts in mind. It was released in 2026 and covers 282 languages across 22 styles.

name: Nuckle
classification_notes: Described explicitly as a functional grotesque built around an increased x-height, with minimal uppercase-to-lowercase differentiation, placing it firmly in the grotesque sans-serif tradition.
summary: A functional grotesque originally drawn in 2011, built on a tall x-height for strong legibility at small sizes without sacrificing display presence.
description: Designed by Filip Matejíček and Jan Charvát, Nuckle predates Heavyweight's formal founding and was substantially revised for its 2026 release. The tall x-height keeps lowercase forms open and readable at small text sizes, while the deliberately narrow gap between uppercase and lowercase cap heights produces a calm, even colour on the page. With 1,095 glyphs across 301 languages and 16 styles, the family is built for broad multilingual deployment.
foundry_blurb: Heavyweight describe Nuckle as their oldest typeface, originally designed in 2011. It is a functional grotesque centred on an increased x-height that delivers exceptional legibility at small sizes while holding presence at display scale. The gap between uppercase and lowercase is kept deliberately minimal, with emphasis placed on clean, readable accents.

name: Cigars
classification_notes: Cigars draws on the Century typeface lineage but its defining characteristic is calligraphic construction, with perpendicular terminal cuts on letters like 'c', 'f' and 'r' rather than classic bracketed or ball terminals.
summary: A calligraphic serif with perpendicular terminal cuts, rooted in the Century typeface tradition and originally drawn for a Prague philosophy conference.
description: Designed by Jan Horčík, Cigars takes its name from old Marlboro advertising and its use of Century, a lineage visible in the overall construction but departed from in the details: terminals on 'c', 'f' and 'r' are cut perpendicularly rather than finished in the classical manner. Version 2.0 expanded the original conference commission into a fuller family, now at ten styles across three weights with coverage for 352 languages. The result is a literary serif with enough warmth for long-form text and enough idiosyncrasy for editorial display work.
foundry_blurb: Heavyweight describe Cigars as one of their earliest serifs, named out of admiration for the Century typeface as used in vintage Marlboro advertising. Originally a custom design for a Prague philosophy conference, it grew into a fuller family in version 2.0. Its key characteristic is a calligraphic quality expressed in perpendicular terminal cuts on letters like 'c', 'f' and 'r', departing from classical terminal conventions.

name: Pano
classification_notes: A low-contrast sans serif with humanist details such as a double-storey 'a', organic 's', and expressive ampersand, distinguishing it from geometric constructions. Smooth arm-to-stem transitions and vertically terminated strokes place it firmly in the humanist sans tradition.
summary: A wide-proportioned humanist sans originating from a racing bike logotype, offered in four styles suited primarily to display and headline use.
description: Drawn by Filip Matejíček and released in 2015, Pano grew from a logotype commission for a cycling brand, its notably wide proportions a direct inheritance from that brief. The design pairs low contrast and vertically terminated strokes with humanist details including a double-storey 'a', a characterful 's', and an expressive ampersand, keeping it distinct from geometric sans families. A high x-height extends its usefulness into short text settings, though its strongest register remains poster, book title, and magazine spread work.
foundry_blurb: Pano began as a logotype for a racing bike brand, which accounts for its characteristic width. It is a low-contrast sans with smooth transitions from arms to stems and vertically cut stroke endings, given a humanist character through details like the 'a', 's', and ampersand. Designed by Filip Matejíček, spaced and kerned by Heavyweight Digital Type Foundry, it was released in 2015 and covers 262 languages across six styles including Light, Regular, Bold, and Italics.

name: Boxi
designer: Jan Horčík, Giorgia Demi, Jan Charvát
classification_notes: Boxi is a display typeface rooted in folk lettering tradition, specifically the improvised tape-and-knife repairs of fallen plexiglass shop signs in Spain. Its deliberate imperfection and mild inconsistency place it firmly in the vernacular display category.
summary: A display typeface derived from the improvised tape-and-knife letterforms found on repaired Spanish commercial signs, treating mild inconsistency as a deliberate formal quality.
description: Boxi descends from Heavyweight's Animo, which drew from Spanish rótulos signage, but pushes into more specific folk territory: the ad hoc repair of fallen plexiglass shop letters using tape, sometimes cut further with a craft knife. That improvisational gesture becomes the typeface's organising principle, with irregularity treated as a feature rather than a defect. Across 11 styles, 1,008 glyphs and support for 356 languages, it covers a wide range of use cases while retaining the rawness of its source material.
foundry_blurb: Heavyweight describe Boxi as a display typeface derived from Animo, itself inspired by Spanish commercial rótulos signs. The specific origin point is the folk improvisation that occurred when a plexiglass letter fell from a sign and a shop owner replaced it with tape, sometimes refining the shape further with a craft knife. Boxi takes that gesture as its starting point, treating imperfection and mild inconsistency not as flaws but as the defining quality of the design.

name: Left
classification_notes: Left features subtle, almost invisible serifs on the left side of each stem only, making it a distinctive semi-serif or partial-serif design that occupies the boundary between sans and serif classification. The serifs function as a typographic tension device rather than a conventional stroke-finishing element.
summary: A semi-serif text typeface that places barely-visible serifs on the left side of each stem only, creating a subliminal visual tension that registers in the reading experience without being consciously noticed.
description: Designed by Filip Matejíček and released by Heavyweight in 2014, Left carries serifs on only one side of each stem, a device so restrained that readers feel rather than see the effect. With four styles covering 463 glyphs across 260 languages, the family is narrow in weight range but precise in intent. Monopol magazine is among its most prominent users, situating it firmly in European editorial contexts.
foundry_blurb: Heavyweight describe Left as one of their evergreens, with roots going back to 2013. Its defining characteristic is the addition of subtle, almost invisible serifs on the left side of each stem, giving text a quiet tension the eye feels without registering. The family covers 260 languages in 4 styles and counts Monopol magazine among its most prominent uses.

name: Atlantic
classification_notes: Classified as a display sans serif by the foundry itself; stems broaden at terminals in place of serifs, giving a calligraphic quality akin to a flared or optically-serifed grotesque at display sizes.
summary: A display sans with broadening stems in place of serifs, defined by a calligraphic quality and a pronounced diagonal axis on round letterforms.
description: Originating as a single thin style made for a jewellery designer, Atlantic grew into a 28-style family covering 356 languages and 1,466 glyphs. Its distinguishing feature is the way stems broaden at terminals rather than terminating in conventional serifs, producing a calligraphic warmth that reads distinctly at display scale. The family has been used editorially in New York Magazine and El País, and commercially by M.A.C Cosmetics.
foundry_blurb: Heavyweight describe Atlantic as a display sans where notional serifs are replaced with broadening stems, carrying a calligraphic quality and a distinctly diagonal axis on round letterforms. Designed by Jan Horčík and Arnaud Chemin, it began as a single thin style for a jewellery designer and later expanded into a full family of 28 styles, released in 2022.
